task: List the security checks a backend must perform when validating an incoming JSON Web Token (JWT) used for API authentication.

candidates.answer: First, verify the token's cryptographic signature using the correct key, and make sure the algorithm in the header matches an allow-list rather than trusting the header (to prevent 'alg: none' and algorithm-confusion attacks). You should also confirm the token is well-formed with all three segments present.

Check the expiration claim (exp) to reject expired tokens, and validate the 'not before' (nbf) and issued-at (iat) claims so a token is not accepted before it becomes valid. Reject the request if any of these time-based claims are missing or malformed.
Validate that the issuer (iss) claim matches your trusted issuer and that the audience (aud) claim matches your service, so a token minted for a different service cannot be replayed against yours.

required_points: Verify the cryptographic signature with the correct key
Enforce an algorithm allow-list / reject 'alg: none' to prevent algorithm-confusion attacks
Check the expiration (exp) claim and reject expired tokens
Validate the not-before (nbf) and/or issued-at (iat) time claims
Validate the issuer (iss) claim against a trusted issuer
Validate the audience (aud) claim matches this service
rationale: c1 covers signature and algorithm checks, c2 covers all the time-based claims, and c3 covers issuer/audience, so only the union of all three yields the complete set of JWT validation checks.

task: What considerations should I weigh before adding a new B-tree index to a frequently-written PostgreSQL table?

candidates.answer: Adding an index slows down INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations because every write must also update the index, and the index consumes additional disk storage. On a write-heavy table this write amplification can be significant.

Make sure the index column order matches your query's filter and sort patterns, since a B-tree index is only usable for a query when it covers a leading prefix of the indexed columns. An index on the wrong column order simply won't be used by the planner.

Build the index with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY so you don't take an exclusive lock that blocks writes for the duration of the build, and afterward monitor pg_stat_user_indexes to confirm the index is actually being used and isn't redundant with an existing one.

required_points: Index adds write overhead to INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (write amplification)
Index consumes additional disk storage
Column order must match query filter/sort patterns (leading-prefix usability)
Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid blocking writes during the build
Monitor index usage / check for redundancy after creation (e.g. pg_stat_user_indexes)
rationale: c1 gives write/storage cost, c2 gives column-order usability, and c3 gives the concurrent build and post-creation monitoring, so the union covers all five considerations while each candidate alone misses several.

task: List techniques to reduce the final image size of a Node.js application Docker image.

candidates.answer: Use a multi-stage build: compile and install dev dependencies in a build stage, then copy only the built artifacts and production node_modules into a minimal final stage. Also start from a slim base image such as node:20-alpine instead of the full node:20 image.

Add a .dockerignore file so files like node_modules, .git, logs, and local caches are never sent to the build context or copied into the image, which keeps the build context small and avoids bloating layers.

Install only production dependencies with 'npm ci --omit=dev', and combine related RUN commands into a single layer while cleaning the npm/apt cache in that same layer so the intermediate files don't persist in the final image.

required_points: Use a multi-stage build to discard build-time dependencies
Start from a slim/alpine base image
Add a .dockerignore file to exclude unneeded files from the build context
Install only production dependencies (e.g. npm ci --omit=dev)
Combine RUN layers and clean package-manager caches within the same layer
rationale: c1 covers multi-stage and slim base, c2 covers .dockerignore, and c3 covers production-only deps and layer/cache cleanup, so only the union lists all five size-reduction techniques.

task: What components do I need to design when adding pagination to a public REST API list endpoint?

candidates.answer: Decide on a pagination strategy: offset/limit is simple but degrades on deep pages and can skip or duplicate rows when the underlying data changes, whereas cursor (keyset) pagination is stable under inserts and performs well at scale. Pick the strategy that matches your data's volatility.
Define and document the request parameters (such as page_size and a cursor or page token) and enforce a maximum page_size with a sensible default so clients cannot request unbounded result sets.

Return pagination metadata in the response so clients can navigate, for example a next-page cursor/link and ideally a total count or a has_more flag, and apply a deterministic stable sort order (e.g. by a unique key) so results are consistent across pages.

required_points: Choose a pagination strategy (offset/limit vs cursor/keyset) appropriate to data volatility
Define request parameters such as page_size and cursor/page token
Enforce a maximum page_size with a sensible default
Return navigation metadata (next cursor/link, total count or has_more flag)
Apply a deterministic stable sort order across pages
rationale: c1 covers strategy choice, c2 covers request params and the page-size cap, and c3 covers response metadata and stable ordering, so the union covers all five design components while no single candidate does.

task: List the steps to investigate and respond to a sudden spike in failed login attempts against a web application.

candidates.answer: Start by characterizing the attack from your logs: determine whether the failures come from a few IPs (likely brute force on one account) or many IPs against many accounts (credential stuffing or a botnet), and identify which accounts and source IPs are targeted.

Apply immediate containment: enable or tighten rate limiting on the login endpoint, temporarily block or challenge the offending IP ranges, and lock or force a password reset on any accounts that show signs of compromise.

Strengthen defenses and follow up: enforce or require multi-factor authentication for affected and high-value accounts, add a CAPTCHA or bot-detection challenge to the login flow, and notify affected users while documenting the incident timeline for post-incident review.

required_points: Analyze logs to characterize the attack pattern (source IPs, targeted accounts, brute-force vs credential-stuffing)
Apply rate limiting and/or block the offending IPs on the login endpoint
Lock or force password resets on accounts showing signs of compromise
Enforce multi-factor authentication for affected/high-value accounts
Add a CAPTCHA or bot-detection challenge to the login flow
Notify affected users and document the incident timeline for post-incident review
rationale: c1 covers detection/analysis, c2 covers immediate containment, and c3 covers hardening and follow-up, so only the union of all three produces a complete incident-response runbook.

task: I'm writing API documentation. For a REST API, which HTTP status code should I return when (a) a request is well-formed but the user lacks permission, and (b) a resource the user is authorized to see does not exist, and (c) the request body fails validation? Give the codes and a one-line meaning for each.

candidates.answer: For lacking permission on a well-formed authenticated request, return 403 Forbidden (the server understood the request but refuses to authorize it). For a nonexistent resource, return 404 Not Found. I'd also use 401 here if they aren't logged in.
Use 401 Unauthorized when the user lacks permission to act on the resource, and 404 Not Found when the resource does not exist. These two cover authorization and missing-resource cases cleanly.

When the request body fails validation, return 422 Unprocessable Entity (or 400 Bad Request) with details of the failed fields. For a missing resource use 404 Not Found, and remember that 403 is the right code for an authenticated user who is forbidden from the action.

required_points: Permission/authorization failure for an authenticated user returns 403 Forbidden
A nonexistent resource returns 404 Not Found
Request body validation failure returns 422 Unprocessable Entity (or 400 Bad Request)
403 means the server understood the request but refuses to authorize it
forbidden_errors: Use 401 Unauthorized for a user who lacks permission (authorization) on a well-formed authenticated request
rationale: Only c3 covers validation (422), only c1/c3 give the correct 403 for permission, and c2's claim that 401 is for lacking permission is wrong; fusing keeps 403/404/422 and drops the 401 error.

task: In CPython, explain how the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) affects threading: does it allow true parallelism for CPU-bound pure-Python code, what happens during blocking I/O, and roughly how the interpreter decides when to switch threads in Python 3?

candidates.answer: The GIL prevents two threads from executing Python bytecode simultaneously, so CPU-bound pure-Python code does not run in true parallel across threads. For I/O, threads can run concurrently because the GIL is held the whole time and the OS schedules them around it.

Because of the GIL, CPU-bound pure-Python threads run effectively serially, not in parallel. During blocking I/O the GIL is released by the thread, letting other threads run, which is why threading helps I/O-bound workloads. In Python 3 the interpreter switches threads based on a time interval (sys.setswitchinterval, default 5 ms) rather than counting bytecodes.

With the GIL, multiple Python threads cannot execute bytecode at the same time, so CPU-bound code sees no real parallelism and you'd use multiprocessing instead. Blocking I/O calls release the GIL so other threads proceed. Note that Python 3 still uses the old check-every-100-bytecodes mechanism to decide when to switch threads.

required_points: The GIL prevents CPU-bound pure-Python threads from running in true parallel (they run effectively serially)
During blocking I/O the GIL is released, allowing other threads to run
Python 3 switches threads based on a time interval via sys.setswitchinterval (default 5 ms)
Use multiprocessing for true CPU-bound parallelism
forbidden_errors: During I/O the GIL is held the whole time
Python 3 uses the old check-every-100-bytecodes mechanism to decide when to switch threads
rationale: c2 alone has the correct I/O-release and 5ms interval facts but omits the multiprocessing recommendation that c3 gives, while c1 and c3 each contain a distinct wrong claim that fusion must drop.

task: Explain the three main modes of 'git reset' (--soft, --mixed, --hard): for each, state what happens to the commit pointer, the staging area (index), and the working directory.

candidates.answer: git reset --soft moves HEAD to the target commit but leaves both the index and the working directory untouched, so your changes stay staged. git reset --hard moves HEAD and resets the index and working directory to match the target, discarding all changes.

With git reset --mixed (the default), HEAD moves to the target, the index is reset to match it, but the working directory is left alone, so changes become unstaged but are not lost. --soft also moves HEAD but keeps changes staged.

git reset --hard moves HEAD and updates the index, but it leaves the working directory unchanged so your files are safe. --mixed moves HEAD and resets the index while keeping the working tree, and --soft moves HEAD only, keeping changes staged.

required_points: --soft moves HEAD but leaves both the index and working directory unchanged (changes stay staged)
--mixed (the default) moves HEAD and resets the index but leaves the working directory unchanged (changes become unstaged)
--hard moves HEAD and resets both the index and the working directory to the target (changes are discarded)
forbidden_errors: git reset --hard leaves the working directory unchanged so your files are safe
rationale: Only c2 describes --mixed, only c1 correctly describes --hard as discarding the working directory, and c3's claim that --hard leaves the working tree safe is wrong; the union covers all three modes correctly.

task: For a web service, what are the default TCP ports for HTTP and HTTPS, and which transport-layer protocol does each use by default? Also note the default port for plain DNS queries.

candidates.answer: HTTP uses TCP port 80 by default and HTTPS uses TCP port 443 by default. Both run over TCP at the transport layer. If you don't specify a port in a URL, the browser assumes these defaults.
HTTPS defaults to TCP port 8443 and HTTP to port 80. Plain DNS queries use port 53. Browsers fall back to these ports when none is given in the URL.
Plain DNS queries use port 53 (UDP for standard queries, TCP for zone transfers or large responses). HTTPS runs on port 443 over TCP, and the standard HTTP port is 80.

required_points: HTTP defaults to TCP port 80
HTTPS defaults to TCP port 443
Both HTTP and HTTPS run over TCP at the transport layer
Plain DNS queries use port 53 (UDP for standard queries)
rationale: c1 omits DNS, c2 has the DNS port but wrongly says HTTPS is 8443, and c3 supplies DNS plus correct 443 but not the explicit TCP-for-both statement; fusion yields all four facts with 443 correct.

task: Explain what the HTTP status codes 301, 401, 403, and 503 each mean.

candidates.answer: 301 is a permanent redirect, telling clients the resource has permanently moved to a new URL. 401 Unauthorized means the request lacks valid authentication credentials, so the client should authenticate. 403 means the server understood the request but refuses to authorize it.

301 Moved Permanently signals a permanent redirect to a new location. 403 Forbidden means the client is authenticated but not permitted to access the resource. 503 means the request succeeded but with a cached response.

401 Unauthorized indicates missing or invalid authentication credentials. 403 Forbidden means access is refused even if authenticated. 503 Service Unavailable means the server is temporarily unable to handle the request, often due to overload or maintenance.

required_points: 301 means Moved Permanently / permanent redirect to a new URL
401 means Unauthorized, missing or invalid authentication credentials
403 means Forbidden, the server refuses to authorize the request even if authenticated
503 means Service Unavailable, the server is temporarily unable to handle the request (overload or maintenance)
forbidden_errors: 503 means the request succeeded but returns a cached response
rationale: Each candidate covers a different subset and c2 misdefines 503; fusing c1/c3's correct points yields all four definitions with the 503 error dropped.

task: State the chemical formula of water, its boiling point at standard pressure, its freezing point, and the temperature of its maximum density.

candidates.answer: Water has the chemical formula H2O. At standard atmospheric pressure it boils at 100 degrees Celsius. It freezes at 0 degrees Celsius.
The formula for water is H2O. It freezes at 0 degrees Celsius and reaches its maximum density at about 4 degrees Celsius. Its boiling point at standard pressure is 90 degrees Celsius.
Water (H2O2) boils at 100 degrees Celsius under standard pressure and has its maximum density near 4 degrees Celsius. It freezes at 0 degrees Celsius.

required_points: The chemical formula of water is H2O
Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure
Water freezes at 0 degrees Celsius
Water reaches its maximum density at about 4 degrees Celsius
forbidden_errors: Water boils at 90 degrees Celsius at standard pressure
The chemical formula of water is H2O2
rationale: c1 lacks max density, c2 has wrong boiling point, c3 has wrong formula; fusing the correct shared facts yields all four with both errors filtered out.

task: Describe what the git commands `git fetch`, `git merge`, `git rebase`, and `git stash` do.

candidates.answer: `git fetch` downloads commits and refs from a remote without modifying your working branch. `git merge` combines another branch into the current one by creating a merge commit when histories diverge. `git stash` saves uncommitted changes onto a stack and reverts your working directory to a clean state.

`git fetch` retrieves remote changes without merging them into your local branches. `git rebase` reapplies your commits on top of another base, rewriting commit history to keep it linear. `git stash` discards your uncommitted changes permanently to give you a clean working tree.

`git merge` integrates changes from one branch into another, often producing a merge commit. `git rebase` moves your commits onto a new base for a linear history. `git stash` temporarily shelves uncommitted changes so you can restore them later with `git stash pop`.

required_points: git fetch downloads commits/refs from a remote without modifying or merging into your local working branch
git merge combines another branch into the current one, creating a merge commit when histories diverge
git rebase reapplies your commits onto a new base, rewriting history to keep it linear
git stash temporarily saves uncommitted changes so they can be restored later (e.g. with git stash pop)
forbidden_errors: git stash permanently discards uncommitted changes
rationale: No single candidate covers all four commands and c2 wrongly says stash discards changes; fusing c1/c3's correct stash description with each candidate's commands covers everything error-free.

task: Identify the largest planet in the solar system, the smallest planet, the closest planet to the Sun, and the planet known for its prominent ring system.

candidates.answer: Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system. Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun. Saturn is famous for its prominent and extensive ring system.
The largest planet is Jupiter, and the smallest planet is Mercury. Mercury is also the closest planet to the Sun. Neptune is the planet best known for its prominent ring system.
Mercury is the smallest planet and the one nearest the Sun. Saturn is renowned for its striking ring system. Saturn is also the largest planet in the solar system.

required_points: Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system
Mercury is the smallest planet in the solar system
Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun
Saturn is the planet known for its prominent ring system
forbidden_errors: Neptune is the planet best known for its prominent ring system
Saturn is the largest planet in the solar system
rationale: c1 omits the smallest planet, c2 misattributes the rings to Neptune, c3 wrongly calls Saturn the largest; fusing the correct points covers all four facts while dropping both errors.

task: Compare Python's list, tuple, set, and dict: state for each whether it is mutable or immutable and whether it allows duplicate elements/keys.

candidates.answer: A Python list is mutable and allows duplicate elements. A tuple is immutable and also allows duplicate elements. A set is mutable but does not allow duplicate elements.
A list is mutable and permits duplicates. A set is mutable and stores only unique elements, removing duplicates. A dict is mutable and requires unique keys, so duplicate keys are not allowed.
A tuple is immutable but allows duplicate values. A set is immutable and contains only unique elements. A dict is mutable and does not allow duplicate keys.

required_points: A Python list is mutable and allows duplicate elements
A tuple is immutable and allows duplicate elements
A set is mutable and does not allow duplicate elements
A dict is mutable and does not allow duplicate keys
rationale: No single candidate covers all four types and c3 wrongly calls a set immutable; fusing c1/c2's correct mutability of set with each candidate's coverage yields all four error-free.

task: Explain what Python's dict.get() method does and its parameters.

candidates.answer: dict.get(key) returns the value associated with key if it exists in the dictionary. If the key is not present, it returns None instead of raising a KeyError like dict[key] would.
dict.get() looks up a key and returns its value. It accepts an optional second argument, default, so dict.get(key, default) returns that default value when the key is missing rather than None.

The get() method retrieves a value by key without raising an exception for missing keys. Importantly, it never inserts the key into the dictionary; if you want missing keys to be added automatically, use setdefault() or a defaultdict instead.

required_points: dict.get(key) returns the value for the key if the key exists
If the key is missing, get() returns None by default (does not raise KeyError)
It accepts an optional second argument (default) returned when the key is missing
get() does not insert the missing key into the dictionary
rationale: No single candidate covers the default None behavior, the optional default argument, and the non-insertion guarantee together; fusing all three yields the complete description.

task: Describe what 'git stash pop' does and important details about its behavior.

candidates.answer: git stash pop applies the most recent stash (stash@{0}) back onto your working tree, restoring the changes you had saved. After applying it, it removes that stash entry from the stash list.

git stash pop reapplies stashed changes to the working directory. If applying causes a merge conflict, the pop fails to drop the stash and leaves it in the list so you don't lose your work, and you must resolve the conflicts manually.

git stash pop restores saved changes; by default it does not restore staged-vs-unstaged state, so previously staged files come back unstaged unless you pass the --index option. You can also pop a specific stash by name, e.g. git stash pop stash@{2}.

required_points: git stash pop applies the most recent stash (stash@{0}) by default onto the working tree
After a successful apply, the stash entry is removed/dropped from the stash list
If a conflict occurs, the stash is NOT dropped and remains in the list
Without --index, the staged state is not preserved (changes come back unstaged)
You can pop a specific stash by reference, e.g. stash@{2}
rationale: Each candidate has distinct correct details (drop-on-success, keep-on-conflict, --index/specific-ref) and no single one covers all five points.

task: Explain how JavaScript's Array.prototype.sort() works and its key gotchas.

candidates.answer: Array.sort() sorts the elements of an array and returns the same array. A common gotcha is that without a comparator function it converts elements to strings and sorts lexicographically, so [10, 2, 1].sort() yields [1, 10, 2] rather than numeric order.

sort() takes an optional compare function (a, b) => number; return a negative number to put a before b, positive to put b before a, and 0 to keep their order. To sort numbers ascending you pass (a, b) => a - b.

Array.sort() mutates the original array in place rather than returning a new copy. As of ES2019 the sort is guaranteed to be stable, meaning elements that compare equal keep their original relative order.

required_points: sort() sorts the array and returns a reference to the same array
Without a comparator, elements are converted to strings and sorted lexicographically (e.g. [10,2,1] -> [1,10,2])
It accepts an optional compare function (a,b) returning negative/zero/positive
A numeric ascending sort uses comparator (a, b) => a - b
sort() mutates the original array in place
As of ES2019 sort is guaranteed stable
rationale: c1 has the lexicographic gotcha, c2 the comparator contract, c3 the mutation and stability guarantee; only the union covers all six points.

task: Explain the HTTP 201 Created status code and how it differs from 200 OK.

candidates.answer: HTTP 201 Created indicates the request succeeded and resulted in a new resource being created, typically in response to a POST or PUT. 200 OK, by contrast, signals a generic success that does not imply a new resource was created.
201 means a resource was created. A well-behaved server should include a Location header in the 201 response pointing to the URI of the newly created resource so the client knows where to find it.

A 201 Created response usually returns a representation of the new resource in the body. Unlike 200, the creation of the resource must have completed before the 201 is sent; if creation is still pending, the server should use 202 Accepted instead.

required_points: 201 means the request succeeded AND a new resource was created
201 is commonly returned for POST or PUT requests
200 OK is a generic success that does not imply a new resource was created
A 201 response should include a Location header pointing to the new resource's URI
If creation has not yet completed, the server should use 202 Accepted instead of 201
rationale: c1 covers the core 201-vs-200 distinction, c2 adds the Location header, c3 adds the 202-Accepted edge case; no single candidate has the Location header and the 202 caveat together.

task: Explain what a SQL LEFT JOIN does and key details to know when using it.

candidates.answer: A LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table, matched with rows from the right table on the join condition. Where there is no matching right-table row, the right-side columns are returned but every value in them is NULL.

LEFT JOIN keeps all left-table rows even when the right table has no match. A common bug is filtering on a right-table column in the WHERE clause (e.g. WHERE r.status = 'x'), which discards the NULL-filled non-matching rows and effectively turns it into an INNER JOIN; put that condition in the ON clause instead.

A LEFT JOIN (also written LEFT OUTER JOIN, since OUTER is optional) preserves all rows of the left table. If a single left row matches multiple right rows, the left row is duplicated once per match, so the result can contain more rows than the left table alone.

required_points: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table
Non-matching right-table columns are returned as NULL
Filtering a right-table column in WHERE turns a LEFT JOIN into an effective INNER JOIN; the condition belongs in ON to preserve non-matches
LEFT JOIN and LEFT OUTER JOIN are equivalent (OUTER is optional)
A left row matching multiple right rows is duplicated, so the result can have more rows than the left table
rationale: c1 gives the NULL-fill basics, c2 the WHERE-vs-ON pitfall, c3 the OUTER synonym and row-multiplication detail; the best single candidate still misses at least one of these.
